title: The Keynesian multiplier
summary: An HL-only, Paper 3 quantitative topic. Because one person's spending is another person's income, an initial injection into the circular flow sets off successive rounds of re-spending, so the final change in national income is larger than the injection that started it. This lesson builds the multiplier from the marginal propensities, derives the two forms of the formula k = 1/(1 − MPC) = 1/MPW, works numerical examples the way Paper 3 marks them, and draws out what the multiplier means for fiscal policy.
learningObjectives: Explain the multiplier as the process by which an initial injection leads to a larger final change in national income.
Define the marginal propensities to consume, save, tax and import, and derive the marginal propensity to withdraw (MPW = MPS + MPT + MPM).
Calculate the multiplier using k = 1/(1 − MPC) = 1/MPW, and calculate the final change in real GDP from a given injection.
Explain the significance of the multiplier for the effectiveness of fiscal policy.
levelNote: HL only — Higher Level content assessed on Paper 3.

simpleExplanation.title: Why one dollar of spending creates more than one dollar of income
simpleExplanation.summary: An initial injection of spending does not stop when it is first received. Each recipient spends part of it, which becomes someone else's income, who spends part of that, and so on. Adding up every round, national income rises by a multiple of the original injection — that multiple is the multiplier.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of a single $100 note handed to a builder by the government. The builder keeps some and spends part of it at a café; the café owner spends part of that at a shop; the shopkeeper spends part again. The same money keeps changing hands, and every hand it passes through counts as new income. The total income created is far larger than the original $100 — but it is not infinite, because at each round some money leaks away into savings, taxes and imports.
steps: An injection (government spending, investment or exports) enters the circular flow of income.
Households receive it as income and spend a fraction — the marginal propensity to consume (MPC) — while the rest leaks out as savings, taxes and imports.
That spending becomes another group's income, and the process repeats in ever-smaller rounds.
Summing all the rounds gives a final change in income equal to the injection multiplied by k = 1/(1 − MPC) = 1/MPW.

flashcards.back: The process by which an initial injection into the circular flow leads to a larger final change in national income, because spending in each round becomes income that is partly re-spent in the next.
An addition of spending into the circular flow that does not originate from household income: Investment (I), Government spending (G) and Exports (X).
Income that is not passed on in the circular flow: Savings (S), Taxes (T) and spending on imports (M). Withdrawals are what make the multiplier finite.
The fraction of an additional unit of income that households spend on domestic goods and services. MPC = ΔC / ΔY.
The fraction of an additional unit of income that households save. MPS = ΔS / ΔY.
The fraction of an additional unit of income paid to the government in taxes. MPT = ΔT / ΔY.
The fraction of an additional unit of income spent on imported goods and services. MPM = ΔM / ΔY.
The fraction of an additional unit of income that leaks out of the circular flow. MPW = MPS + MPT + MPM.
In a closed economy with no government, income is either consumed or saved, so MPC + MPS = 1. Once tax and imports are added, MPC + MPW = 1 instead.

flashcards.front: The two forms of the multiplier formula
flashcards.back: k = 1/(1 − MPC) and k = 1/MPW. They are identical because 1 − MPC equals MPW (everything not consumed is withdrawn).
ΔY = injection × k. For a change in government spending, ΔY = ΔG × k.

flashcards.front: Exam trap: which formula uses MPW?
flashcards.back: k = 1/MPW, NOT 1/(1 − MPW). Use 1/(1 − MPC) with the propensity to CONSUME, or 1/MPW with the propensity to WITHDRAW. Mixing them up is the most common Paper 3 error.

flashcards.front: Why a bigger MPC gives a bigger multiplier
flashcards.back: A larger MPC means more of each round is re-spent and less leaks away, so 1 − MPC (the denominator) is smaller and k is larger.

quickCheck.prompt: The correct formula for the Keynesian multiplier is:
In a simple closed economy with no government, if MPC = 0.6 then MPS must equal:
The marginal propensity to withdraw (MPW) is correctly defined as:
Two economies are identical except that Economy A has a higher MPC than Economy B. Compared with Economy B, Economy A's multiplier is:
An economy has MPS = 0.15, MPT = 0.15 and MPM = 0.20. Its multiplier is:

faq.q: Why are the two multiplier formulas the same thing?
faq.a: Every extra unit of income is either consumed domestically or withdrawn (saved, taxed, or spent on imports). So MPC + MPW = 1, which means 1 − MPC = MPW. Substituting one for the other, 1/(1 − MPC) and 1/MPW are algebraically identical. Use whichever the data gives you: if the question states the MPC, use 1/(1 − MPC); if it lists MPS, MPT and MPM, add them to get MPW and use 1/MPW.

faq.q: Why is the real-world multiplier often smaller than the calculated one?
faq.a: The formula assumes the propensities stay constant, that there are no time lags, and that spare capacity lets output rise without simply pushing up prices. In practice re-spending takes time, households may save more of a windfall if they are uncertain, and if the economy is near full employment extra demand raises the price level rather than real GDP. Deficit-financed government spending can also cause some crowding out of private investment. These frictions mean the observed change in real GDP is usually less than the textbook multiplier predicts.

faq.q: Does the multiplier work in reverse?
faq.a: Yes. A fall in an injection — for example a cut in government spending or a drop in investment — sets off the same chain in reverse, producing a final fall in national income that is larger than the initial withdrawal. This 'downward' or 'reverse' multiplier is one reason recessions can deepen and helps explain why austerity during a downturn can be contractionary by more than the headline cut.

sections.content: The Keynesian multiplier is Higher Level content and is assessed on Paper 3, the quantitative paper. That means the examiner is looking not only for the idea but for correct method, accurate figures and clearly shown working. This lesson explains the concept, builds the formula from the marginal propensities, and then works through the exact style of calculation Paper 3 rewards — including how our marking engine awards each mark.
The core idea: an injection becomes rounds of income

The multiplier rests on a single insight: one person's spending is another person's income. When new spending is injected into the economy — say the government spends on building a hospital — the construction workers and suppliers receive it as income. They spend part of that income, which becomes income for shopkeepers and other firms, who spend part again. Each round is smaller than the last because some income leaks away, but adding up every round gives a final rise in national income that is a multiple of the original injection.

items: Injections are Investment (I), Government spending (G) and Exports (X) — spending that enters the circular flow.
Withdrawals (leakages) are Savings (S), Taxes (T) and Imports (M) — income that leaves the flow.
The multiplier is finite because withdrawals shrink each successive round of spending.
The larger the fraction re-spent (the MPC), the more rounds of meaningful spending occur, and the larger the multiplier.

sections.content: A marginal propensity measures how a household splits an ADDITIONAL unit of income. There are four to know. The marginal propensity to consume (MPC = ΔC/ΔY) is the fraction spent on domestic goods and services. The marginal propensity to save (MPS = ΔS/ΔY), the marginal propensity to tax (MPT = ΔT/ΔY) and the marginal propensity to import (MPM = ΔM/ΔY) are the three ways income leaks out. Because every extra unit of income is either consumed domestically or withdrawn, the propensities always sum to one.

The marginal propensity to withdraw collects all three leakages into a single number: MPW = MPS + MPT + MPM. In the simplest model — a closed economy with no government — there is only one leakage, saving, so MPW = MPS and the identity reduces to the familiar MPC + MPS = 1.

The multiplier, k, tells you how many times larger the final change in income is than the injection that caused it. Because 1 − MPC and MPW are the same quantity (both equal the fraction that leaks away), the multiplier can be written in two equivalent forms.

Read the formula for its economics. A high MPC means people re-spend most of each extra unit, so 1 − MPC is small and k is large. Bigger leakages — a higher MPS, MPT or MPM — raise the denominator and shrink the multiplier. This is why an open economy with a government (three leakages) has a smaller multiplier than a simple closed economy (one leakage). Once you have k, the final change in real GDP is simply the injection scaled up by it.

sections.question:
In a closed economy with no government, MPC = 0.8. Investment rises by $30 million.

(a) Calculate the multiplier.
(b) Calculate the final change in real GDP.
sections.solution:
**(a) Multiplier**

- Formula: k = 1/(1 − MPC).
- Substitute: k = 1/(1 − 0.8) = 1/0.2.
- Answer: **k = 5**.

**(b) Final change in real GDP**

- Formula: ΔY = injection × k.
- Substitute: ΔY = $30m × 5.
- Answer: **ΔY = $150 million**.

The $30m of investment ultimately raises national income by $150m because 80 cents of every extra dollar is re-spent in the next round.

sections.question:
An open economy with a government has MPS = 0.1, MPT = 0.2 and MPM = 0.2. Government spending increases by $60 million.

Calculate the final change in real GDP.
sections.solution:
**Step 1 — Marginal propensity to withdraw.**
- MPW = MPS + MPT + MPM = 0.1 + 0.2 + 0.2 = **0.5**.

**Step 2 — Multiplier.**
- k = 1/MPW = 1/0.5 = **2**.

**Step 3 — Final change in real GDP.**
- ΔY = ΔG × k = $60m × 2 = **$120 million**.

The three leakages sum to 0.5, giving a modest multiplier of 2, so the $60m injection raises real GDP by $120m — half the effect of the closed-economy example above, because more income leaks away each round.

sections.content: The multiplier is the reason fiscal policy can move the economy by more than its own headline cost. A government that increases spending or cuts taxes to close a recessionary (deflationary) gap injects demand that is then re-spent, so the final rise in real GDP exceeds the initial injection — a favourable multiplier makes expansionary fiscal policy more powerful. Knowing k also lets a government estimate the injection needed to reach a target level of income: to raise GDP by a desired amount, the required injection is that amount divided by k.

items: A larger multiplier makes any given change in G or T more effective at changing real GDP — useful when closing an output gap.
The multiplier works both ways: cutting spending in a downturn can contract income by more than the cut itself (the reverse multiplier).
The size of k depends on leakages, so economies with high MPS, MPT or MPM get less 'bang' from fiscal stimulus.
Real-world effects are dampened by time lags, inflation near full employment, and possible crowding out — so the actual change in real GDP is usually below the calculated figure.
**Using k = 1/(1 − MPW).** The formula with MPW has NO 'one minus': it is k = 1/MPW. The '1 − ' belongs only with MPC, because 1 − MPC already equals MPW.
**Forgetting to add all three leakages.** MPW = MPS + MPT + MPM. Dropping the tax or import term gives too small a denominator and an inflated multiplier.

**Confusing MPC with the multiplier.** MPC is a propensity between 0 and 1; k = 1/(1 − MPC) is usually greater than 1. Reporting MPC as the answer, or multiplying the injection by MPC, loses the accuracy marks.
**Multiplying by the wrong base.** ΔY is the injection × k, not the whole level of GDP × k, and not the injection × MPC.

**Omitting units, signs or working.** Paper 3 awards method marks for shown working and accuracy marks for the correct figure WITH units ($m). A right number with no working, or the number without units, forfeits marks.
**Assuming MPC + MPS = 1 in an open economy.** That identity only holds in the simple closed model; once tax and imports exist, it is MPC + MPW = 1.

sections.content: Model answer — marked against the real IB Paper 3 scheme

sections.question: Paper 3 (quantitative): In an economy the marginal propensity to consume is 0.75. Calculate the value of the Keynesian multiplier, and the final change in real GDP resulting from a $40 million increase in government spending. [4]
sections.solution:
**Model answer:**

*Multiplier:*
- k = 1/(1 − MPC) = 1/(1 − 0.75) = 1/0.25 = **4**.

*Final change in real GDP:*
- ΔY = ΔG × k = $40m × 4 = **$160 million**.

---
**How our marking engine awards the marks (4 max):**
- **M1 — method:** correct multiplier formula shown, k = 1/(1 − MPC) with the 0.75 substituted in. The working, not just the result, earns this mark.
- **A1 — accuracy:** correct value **k = 4**.
- **M1 — method:** correct method for the final change, ΔY = injection × k, i.e. $40m × k.
- **A1 — accuracy:** correct value **ΔY = $160 million**, stated with units.
- **Follow-through:** if a candidate found the multiplier wrongly (say k = 5) but then correctly did $40m × their k, the second method mark and the accuracy of the final step are still awarded on their figure. Method and follow-through protect you even when one number slips — provided the working is visible.

sections.content: This is exactly what the Practice button does. Type your own worked solution to this question — formula, substitution, and final figure with units — submit it, and our marking engine grades it against this same Paper 3 scheme (M for method, A for accuracy, with follow-through). The single habit that secures full marks on Paper 3: SHOW every line of working and always attach the units.

sections.label: Get a Paper 3 multiplier calculation marked: find k and the final change in real GDP, with method and accuracy scored
